topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my doorstep, stark white against the faded grey of the hallway. A handwritten label, scrawled in looping, overly familiar script, confirmed my suspicion: this was for the person who’d just moved out. I picked it up, the material surprisingly heavy, and the cheap packing tape felt like a deliberate slight. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I took a step back, my gaze fixed on the address, a slow, simmering resentment beginning to coil in my chest. It was such a blatant disregard for my privacy, a clear message that they hadn't bothered to properly clear out their belongings. I dropped the package onto the welcome mat with a sharp thud, the sound echoing in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

When the delivery notification chimed, a knot formed in my stomach. It was a package, addressed to the previous occupant. I approached the door, my hand hovering over the buzzer, a distinct clenching in my fist betraying my mood. Peeking through the peephole, I saw the bulky box, and a wave of irritation washed over me. I pulled the door open just enough to see the plain brown paper. I didn't bother to open it immediately. Instead, I leaned against the doorframe, crossing my arms tightly across my chest, my expression hardening into something decidedly unimpressed. This felt like an unnecessary imposition, a careless intrusion into my space.

[story 3]

I found the package resting on the communal mailbox, a silent declaration of someone else's messy departure. The handwriting on the shipping label was so casual, so utterly unconcerned with decorum, that a flush crept up my neck. I picked it up, the paper crinkling under my fingers, and the sheer thought of receiving something meant for another resident made my breath catch. I walked back to my unit, my pace deliberately slow, every step radiating a stiff displeasure. I didn't even bother to look at the contents; the mere existence of it was enough to set my teeth on edge. I placed it on the kitchen counter with exaggerated care, as if handling something contaminated.
